does: This paper addresses cross-domain offline reinforcement learning with heterogeneous source datasets collected from multiple domains and diverse behavior policies. The authors identify and formalize value misassignment—a problem where value functions incorrectly assign credit to actions across different data sources, undermining both value alignment and data filtering. They propose V2A, a method that unifies value alignment and assignment by jointly optimizing how the agent learns value estimates while filtering data from heterogeneous sources. The approach theoretically bounds the suboptimality gap and empirically demonstrates improved performance over methods that handle only dynamics alignment or value alignment separately on benchmarks with heterogeneous multi-domain offline data.
connects: This work builds on the offline reinforcement learning literature, particularly methods addressing distribution shift through data filtering and domain adaptation. It extends recent cross-domain offline RL approaches that separate dynamics alignment (handling environment transition differences) and value alignment (ensuring consistent reward interpretation). The paper responds to limitations in prior work that assumed homogeneous source data or single behavior policies, drawing on broader transfer learning and multi-task RL principles. It also connects to value-based offline RL methods like conservative Q-learning and behavioral cloning approaches that struggle with out-of-distribution data, while incorporating insights from domain adaptation literature about handling heterogeneous sources.
enables: This work opens directions for more robust offline RL systems that can leverage diverse pre-collected datasets from realistic scenarios. It enables better policy transfer in settings where multiple source domains and policies are available—common in robotics and autonomous systems where data is gathered from varied sources. The unified treatment of value alignment and assignment could inform future work on credibility assessment in heterogeneous data fusion, multi-source transfer learning, and offline RL in non-stationary or multi-agent environments. The theoretical framework around suboptimality bounds may guide design of filtering mechanisms for other heterogeneous offline learning problems, extending applicability beyond single-task reward structures.
